q: What is high availability (HA) and how is redundancy used to achieve it?
a: HA keeps systems/services running with minimal downtime. Redundancy — duplicate components, systems, networks, and sites — eliminates single points of failure so a failure doesn't cause an outage.
Placing redundant systems/backups in physically separate locations so a single local disaster (fire, flood, outage) can't take down both.

q: What does a load balancer do?
a: Distributes incoming traffic across multiple servers to improve performance and availability. Modes include active/active (all working) and active/passive (standby takes over on failure).
Grouping multiple servers to act as one system so that if one node fails, others continue serving — providing redundancy and scalability.
RAID 0 = striping (speed, no redundancy). RAID 1 = mirroring. RAID 5 = striping + 1 parity (survives 1 disk loss). RAID 6 = 2 parity (survives 2). RAID 10 = mirrored + striped (speed + redundancy).
Full = everything. Incremental = only data changed since the LAST backup (fast backup, slow restore). Differential = all data changed since the last FULL backup (slower backup, faster restore).
Keep 3 copies of data, on 2 different media types, with 1 copy stored offsite.
So a disaster (fire, flood, ransomware) affecting the primary site doesn't also destroy the backups — geographic separation protects recoverability.

q: What are snapshots, journaling, and replication?
a: Snapshot = point-in-time copy of a system/volume. Journaling = logging changes to allow recovery to a consistent state. Replication = continuously copying data to another location for availability.

RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how quickly you must restore service after an outage. RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data loss is acceptable, i.e., how far back you can recover (drives backup frequency).
MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) = average time a system runs before failing (reliability). MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) = average time to fix it and restore service.

q: Hot, warm, and cold disaster recovery sites?
a: Hot = fully equipped and running, near-instant failover (most expensive). Warm = equipped but needs setup/data load (hours). Cold = empty space/power only, longest to bring online (cheapest).

q: What is a restoration order and why does it matter?
a: A predetermined sequence for bringing systems back online during recovery, based on dependencies and business importance (e.g., restore network/auth before applications).
Ensuring you have enough people, technology, and infrastructure to handle normal load AND recover from outages — avoiding being overwhelmed during incidents or growth.

q: What is a UPS vs. a generator?
a: A UPS (battery) provides immediate, short-term power to bridge outages and allow safe shutdown/failover. A generator provides long-term backup power for extended outages.
Tabletop exercises (discussion-based walkthrough), failover testing, simulations, and parallel processing (running recovery systems alongside production).

q: What is an access control vestibule (mantrap)?
a: A small space with two interlocking doors where only one opens at a time, preventing tailgating/piggybacking by allowing one person through at a time.
Fences, lighting, bollards (vehicle barriers), signage, security guards, locks, badges, and surveillance cameras (CCTV).

q: Compare infrared, microwave, ultrasonic, and pressure sensors.
a: Detection sensors with different methods: infrared senses heat/motion, microwave/ultrasonic emit waves and detect reflection changes (motion), pressure detects weight/force. Choose based on environment and cost.
An unauthorized person following an authorized person through a secure door. Defended by access control vestibules and security awareness.
An enclosure that blocks electromagnetic signals — used to prevent wireless emanations/eavesdropping or to isolate devices from RF.
Physically isolating a system/network from all other networks (no connection) to protect highly sensitive systems from remote attack.

q: Why does platform/technology diversity build resilience (and its cost)?
a: Using different vendors/technologies/clouds means a single product flaw or provider outage won't take everything down — but it adds complexity and cost to manage.
